draft.introduction: The Books of Jeu survive in a single Coptic manuscript (the Bruce Codex, now in the Bodleian Library, Oxford), likely dating to the third or fourth century. They present Jesus instructing his disciples in the secret rituals, seals, passwords, and invocations needed to ascend through the heavenly realms after death. Jeu is a divine emanation—a “True God” who presides over the celestial treasuries through which the soul must pass. The text is essentially a ritual manual for the afterlife. Each level of heaven has its own guardian, its own seal, its own password. The soul that knows these can pass through; the soul that does not is turned back. This maps directly onto the theosis question: deification here is not abstract theology but practical technique. The initiate who receives these mysteries is, in the Gnostic understanding, already beginning the process of becoming divine—learning the language and signs of the heavenly world so as to be recognized as belonging to it.

translation.text: This is the book of the gnoses of the invisible God, by means of the hidden mysteries that show the way to the chosen race, leading in rest to the life of the Father, in the coming of the saviour, the deliverer of souls who receive the word of life.

Jesus said to his disciples: “Come to me, all of you, and I will reveal to you the mystery of the emanations and their seals, and their invocations for reaching each treasury. For the Father has given me the authority to reveal these things to you, who are the chosen ones.”

“This is the seal of the first treasury: say this name three times. And this is its password. When the guardians of the first treasury see this seal and hear this password, they will withdraw their veils and open the gates, and you will enter in great glory.”

“And when you have passed through the first treasury, you will come to the second. And the guardians of the second treasury will demand of you the seal and the password of their region. Give them this seal and say this password, and they will open to you.”

Jesus said: “Now I will tell you the mystery of the baptisms: the baptism of water, the baptism of fire, and the baptism of the Holy Spirit. These three baptisms are necessary for the one who would enter the treasury of light.”

“The baptism of fire surpasses the baptism of water. And the baptism of the Holy Spirit surpasses the baptism of fire. But the mystery of the spiritual chrism surpasses them all, for it is the seal of the treasury of the great invisible God.”

translator_notes.note: The opening formula—“the book of the gnoses of the invisible God”—signals that this is esoteric knowledge. The “invisible God” is a standard Gnostic designation for the highest deity, distinct from the visible, material creator (the Demiurge). The “chosen race” echoes 1 Peter 2:9 but in a Gnostic context refers specifically to those with the divine spark.

The system of seals, passwords, and guardians has close parallels in Egyptian funerary literature, particularly the Book of the Dead, where the deceased must know the names and attributes of each gate-guardian to pass through the underworld. The Gnostic innovation is to place this framework within a Christian revelatory context, with Jesus as the one who discloses the passwords.

The threefold baptism—water, fire, and Holy Spirit—is also attested in the Pistis Sophia and other Gnostic texts. It likely reflects actual ritual practice in Gnostic communities. The “spiritual chrism” (anointing) that surpasses all three baptisms may correspond to the Valentinian sacrament of the bridal chamber, the highest initiation.
